All right, you're back and I'm back too with a surprise. I kind of fooled you there. I got a bonus lesson for you for cracking the code. This is a really important topic. I didn't cover it. It's kind of outside the box a bit, but it's very important that you understand this. Here's the deal. In the world of sales training, bogus as it might be outside of me, of course, right? You have these different boxes they try to put sales systems in. The two main boxes are called the old model of selling. Please put a graphic here of the old model of selling. On the other side of the equation, you have what's called the new model of selling. Here's the deal. Very quickly, in the old model of selling, what comes to mind is that hard-charging type A over the top, I'm just, ah, I'm there to rip your throat out basically and close at any cost. I like what you saw in the movie Glenn Gary, Glenn Ross, where they would just bang away in the phones and also on some level what you saw in Straton Oatman in the Wolf of Wall Street, but you'll see it's total nonsense in a few seconds. Let me just go through this old model. According to the old model of selling, you break down the sales process into four chunks. This first chunk, which makes up 10% of the overall sale, so to speak, is called building rapport. That's 10%. Then the next one is 20% of the sale, and that's called asking questions or gathering intelligence. Then you have 30% of the sale is called making your presentation, and then 40% is closing the deal. 100% of the sale, right? Divided in that way. Now let's look to the new model of selling, which is called sort of consultative selling, total joke, but I'll explain the whole process. According to consultative selling, 40% of your time is spent building relationships, basically rapport by another name. 30% is called identifying needs, which is gathering intelligence by another name, right? 20% is called offering solutions, meaning presenting, and then 10%, the smallest one of all, is called closing and confirming. Now let me just tell you something here. The bottom line is that both of these are complete and utter nonsense. I'll tell you why. Number one, let's start with closing the sale, which according to the consultative model, I should only be spending 10% of my time doing, getting reality. If I actually did that, I would be broke and living under a bridge. I mean, good Lord, I'm closing literally every second I open up my mouth, not necessarily in the traditional sense, where I'm asking for the order to close a deal, but I'm always closing my prospect on at least something, whether it's in those first four seconds that I'm a person worth listening to, or when I'm asking my intelligence gathering questions that, yes, you should actually open up to me. I'm trustworthy. I'm an expert in my field. So please answer my questions honestly and forthrightedly. And then yes, of course at the end, when I am closing, I ask for the order directly. But I mean, the notion that I'm only closing 10% of the time is truly and utterly ridiculous. And for that matter, spending only 40% of my time closing is the old model says, that's equally ridiculous. I would still be broke and living under a bridge. You get it? Anything less than 100% of the time spent closing and you will end up in the poor house bottom line. Same thing goes for rapport building or building relationships as the new model calls it, right? I mean, you never stop building rapport, ever. Report is not a constant. If you have a break rapport, you can close. So you're constantly building rapport, nurturing relationships, strengthening the long term times, whether it's 10% by one name or 40% by another. The notion that you could cubby hold this into one section of a sale is just complete and utter nonsense as it is with asking questions. One model calls it qualifying and the other calls it identifying needs. But either way, they're the same damn thing, right? And whether it's 20% or 30% doesn't even matter because the entire notion is flawed and ridiculous. You never stop asking questions. You never stop identifying needs ever. You're always trying to gather deeper and deeper levels of intelligence because at the end of the day, that's what's going to allow you to close the tougher sales, bottom line. And that takes us now to making presentations or in the worthless dribble of the new model offering solutions. I mean, really. I'm presenting from literally the first word out of my mouth. We call this strategic preparation with the straight line system, which is to say you prepare yourself for every possible eventuality before you enter a sales encounter. You don't leave things up to chance. You know exactly what you're going to say before you say it and it starts from word number one because you know that those first four seconds are everything as are the minutes and hours that follow them. You get it? You see in real sales, which is straight line sales, we're always doing all things at once. In fact, let's transition it to a split screen for a second and we'll put the straight line syntax on one side of the screen and our straight line graphic on the other. Okay. So starting with the straight line, this is your open when the sale begins. This is your close when the prospect says, yeah, let's do it. Here are your boundaries above and below the line. There's Pluto up here and your anus down here. Not a very good place to be for most of us, right? Anyway. So you're going to step one of the straight line. You must take immediate control of the sale, which be where right here in the straight line graphic in those first four seconds happens very quickly. Now we move over to the straight line syntax. What do we see? Step number two, gathering intelligence over to the straight line graphic. Here it is right after those first four seconds, but still early on in the sale. Then we switch back over to the syntax step three, building rapport simultaneously. Let's put that underneath gathering intelligence because that happened together, right? Back over to the syntax step four. When you're done gathering that intelligence, building massive rapport, you have your straight line transition. That's that little thin line right here now on the straight line graphic. And then after that transition, what happens next? You make your presentation, your straight line presentation. That's where it is on the graphic over here. And that straight line presentation always ends. Look at your syntax by asking for the order, which happens right here. That's the front half of the straight line syntax. But again, all this is, is a visual representation to show you where your primary focus should be at each point in the sales encounter. I mean, yeah, at certain points in the sale, you'll typically be more focused on one particular outcome, like taking control of the sale and gathering intelligence or presenting a solution or building rapport. If you ever forget that you're always doing all of these things at once, then you're setting yourself up to have a really, really tough time there. See, just because you took control doesn't mean you'll automatically maintain control. So again, always remember that once you lose control, the sales basically over. I mean, 15 seconds later, you say something stupid, guess what happens? You're done. The prospect's conscious mind will take over and say, whoa, whoa, whoa, this is not really a person's worth listening to. It's not who I thought it was. And just like that, they will take back control of the sale and you're done. So again, one more time, always remember that when you're in the real world of sales, there's only one true path and it's a straight line from the open to the close and it starts with taking immediate control of the sale. Why? Because it allows you. It opens up the possibility for you to make every sale the same and then slowly but surely line up each of those five core elements of influence that must be in place for your prospect to say yes. And once you do, bam, everything else becomes really, really easy. All right? So that's your bonus lesson. It wraps up module one. Please take your quiz now. When you're done, meet me in the next module and we will plow on. Take care. Thank you.
